content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Let’s break the term Call Stack into two words: Call and Stack.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

JavaScript call stack
• Call is simple to understand. In programming, whenever we invoke or execute a function, we call it a function call.

• Stack can be thought of as a container. Imagine a container where values are stored, executed, and then removed once their execution is complete.
For example, imagine this container.

• First, we call Function 1, so it goes into the container.

• Then we call Function 2, so it is placed on top of Function 1.

• Finally, we call Function 3, so it is placed on top of Function 2.
Now the container holds three functions:
Top
Function 3
Function 2
Function 1
BottomThe stack follows a specific behavior during execution.
Although the functions entered in the order 1 → 2 → 3, they execute in the reverse order.
Since Function 3 was added last, it executes first. Once its execution finishes, it is removed from the stack.
Then Function 2 executes. After it finishes, it is removed.
Finally, Function 1 executes, and after it finishes, it is also removed.
When all functions have completed execution, the stack becomes empty.
Let’s understand this with a code example.
Suppose we create a function named one() and then call it.
function one() {
two();
}one();
The first thing pushed onto the stack is one().
Now, inside one(), we call two().
So two() is pushed onto the stack.
function two() {
three();
}Inside two(), we call three().
Now three() is pushed onto the stack.
At this point, the stack looks like this:
Top
three()
two()
one()
BottomNow let’s see what happens.
The body of three() executes.
Suppose it contains:
console.log("Hello");As soon as "Hello" is printed, the execution of three() is complete.
Therefore, three() is removed from the stack.
Control then returns to two().
If two() has nothing else to execute after calling three(), then two() also finishes execution and is removed from the stack.
Control then returns to one().
If one() also has nothing left to execute, it finishes and is removed from the stack as well.
Finally, the call stack becomes empty.
Let’s see the execution flow.

• Program starts.

• one() is called and pushed onto the stack.

• two() is called from one() and pushed onto the stack.

• three() is called from two() and pushed onto the stack.
Now the stack contains three functions:
Top
three()
two()
one()
BottomEven though they were called in the order:
one() → two() → three()they execute in the reverse order:
three() → two() → one()
• three() executes, prints its output, and is removed.

• two() resumes execution, finishes, and is removed.

• one() resumes execution, finishes, and is removed.

• Finally, the stack itself becomes empty.
Important Concept
The Call Stack follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle.
This means:

• The function that enters the stack last is the first one to execute completely and leave the stack.
So in our example:
Push order:
one()
two()
three()Execution (Pop) order:
three()
two()
one()
This is exactly how a LIFO data structure works.
That’s all for this simple introduction to the JavaScript Call Stack. Understanding this concept makes it much easier to learn the JavaScript Event Loop later.
